perturbation.original_text: MMI warrants providing the Services with due diligence and care in accordance with the specifications set by Dragon Systems. Should MMI not supply the Services as agreed or should the Services become defective within 6 months from their delivery to Dragon Systems, Dragon Systems may at its option require MMI to complete or re-perform the Services within a reasonable period of time, rescind the contract or refuse payment of the compensation in part or in total, notwithstanding any damage claims.
perturbation.changed_text: MMI warrants providing the Services with due diligence and care in accordance with the specifications set by Dragon Systems. Should MMI not supply the Services as agreed or should the Services become defective, Dragon Systems may at its option require MMI to complete or re-perform the Services, notwithstanding any damage claims.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 6-month defect window and the option for Dragon Systems to rescind the contract or refuse payment significantly reduces consumer protection, conflicting with implied warranty laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c.106 § 2-607 outlines the buyer's rights on improper delivery, including rejection and revocation of acceptance. By removing the ability to rescind the contract for defective services and limiting the timeframe to identify defects, the modified text contradicts this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-607 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-607 Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach after Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over Section 2-607 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-607: Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach after Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over Section 2–607. (1) The buyer must pay at the contract rate for any goods accepted. (2) Acceptance of goods by the buyer precludes rejection of the goods accepted and if made with knowledge of a non-conformity cannot be revoked because of it unless the acceptance was on the reasonable assumption that the non-conformity would be seasonably cured but acceptance does not of itself impair any other remedy provided by this Article for non-conformity. (3) Where a tender has been accepted (a) the buyer must within a reasonable time after he discovers or should have discovered any breach notify the seller of breach or be barred from any remedy; and (b) if the claim is one for infringement or the like (subsection (3) of section 2–312) and the buyer is sued as a result of such a breach he must so notify the seller within a reasonable time after he receives notice of the litigation or be barred from any remedy over for liability established by the litigation. (4) The burden is on the buyer to establish any breach with respect to the goods accepted. (5) Where the buyer is sued for breach of a warranty or other obligation for which his seller is answerable over (a) he may give his seller written notice of the litigation. If the notice states that the seller may come in and defend and that if the seller does not do so he will be bo
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows Dragon Systems to rescind the contract or refuse payment if services are defective within 6 months. The changed contract removes both the 6-month window and the ability to rescind the contract or refuse payment, providing less protection to Dragon Systems. The Law Explanation states that M.G.L. c.106 § 2-607 outlines the buyer's rights on improper delivery, including rejection and revocation of acceptance. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Section 2-607, states that acceptance of goods does not impair any other remedy provided by the Article for non-conformity. The changed text weakens the buyer's remedies by removing the right to rescind the contract, contradicting the intent of Section 2-607 to provide remedies for non-conformity.

perturbation.original_text: Payments will be due in US dollars within 30 days after delivery, or when agreed after delivery of installments or the receipt of invoice by Dragon Systems, which ever is later. MMI shall invoice Dragon Systems indicating the performed services in US$ with reference to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments will be due in US dollars at a time to be determined by MMI. MMI shall invoice Dragon Systems without necessarily detailing the performed services.
perturbation.explanation: By giving MMI the power to determine when payments are due without a specific timeframe, and removing the requirement to detail services performed, this weakens the buyer's ability to dispute charges and introduces legal uncertainty, violating principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 dictates that when a contract doesn't specify a time for performance, the time for shipment or delivery shall be a reasonable time. This change gives MMI arbitrary control over payment timing, undermining the 'reasonable time' standard and the buyer's ability to plan financially.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified payment terms of 'within 30 days after delivery, or when agreed after delivery of installments or the receipt of invoice,' providing a clear timeframe. The changed text, however, states payments are due 'at a time to be determined by MMI,' eliminating this timeframe and violating UCC § 2-309. The law snippet states, 'The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.' By removing the defined timeframe, the modified contract potentially allows for an unreasonable payment timeline dictated solely by MMI, thus creating a contradiction.
